coredata.dc:title: Innovative tumor interstitial fluid-triggered carbon dot-docetaxel nanoassemblies for targeted drug delivery and imaging of HER2-positive breast cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dan
Guo, Dongnan
Zhang, Jing
Tan, Xueping
Deng, Zijie
Hou, Xiaofang
Wang, Sicen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have developed an innovative pH-triggered nanomedicine delivery system, targeting HER2-positive breast cancer cells for effective low-cost, imaging-guided drug delivery and precise therapy. The key feature of this system lies in its unique tumor interstitial fluid microenvironment-responsive drug release behavior which achieved tumor site-specific drug delivery. Our in vitro experiments demonstrated that the carbon dot-integrated material achieves more efficient DTX release (96.13 % at 72 h) in the tumor interstitial fluid microenvironment (pH 6.5), thereby boosting drug concentration at the tumor site and enhancing therapeutic efficacy. Further cell experiments confirmed the system's significant inhibitory effect on HER2-positive tumor cells SKBR3 in a pH 6.5 environment, and apoptosis assays indicating a notable increase in early cell apoptosis (from 8.39 % to 24.61 % compared with pH 7.4). Furthermore, the integration of HER2 aptamer within the carbon dot-based system enables targeted recognition and binding to tumor cells, ensuring more precise delivery of DTX while minimizing potential side effects. Crucially, the carbon dots in this system emit superior red fluorescence (the QY = 47.64 % excited at 535 nm compared with Rodamine 6G), enabling real-time visualization of the drug delivery process. This feature provides valuable feedback on treatment effectiveness, facilitating necessary adjustments. The small size (1.88 ± 0.48 nm) of carbon dots significantly improved their ability to penetrate biological barriers, while their low toxicity (no significant cell toxicity under 350 μg/mL) contributed to the formulation’s outstanding biocompatibility. Overall, this carbon dot-enhanced drug delivery system offers immense potential for enhancing drug efficacy, minimizing side effects, and providing real-time treatment monitoring, thus proposing a innovate strategy for breast cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH-triggered drug release
HER2 targeting
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX, HPRCD@DTX and the QY determination of RCDs
DTX loading efficiency and release behavior of PRCD@DTX
Preparation, characterization, and spectroscopic properties of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX
Morphology and optical properties of RCDs
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis of RCDs
Characterization of PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX
DTX loading efficiency and pH-Triggered DTX release from PRCD@DTX in vitro

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.82273891, 81973277, 82003709), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M643681) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No: 2021JM-039) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of RCDs. (a) TEM image of RCDs, scale bar 20 nm. The left inset shows the size distribution of RCDs and the right inset shows HR-TEM image of RCDs, scale bar was 2 nm. (b) Absorption (blue) spectrum, fluorescence emission spectra in water (red) and in EtOH (black) of RCDs respectively. The inset shows RCDs under natural light (left) and 525 nm light (right), respectively. (c) QY determination: linear plots of absorption and fluorescence intensity RCDs and standard sample (Rhodamine 6G) both in EtOH. (d) FTIR spectrum of RCDs. (e) Fluorescence microscopic and confocal microscopic images of SKBR-3 cells after incubation with RCDs (200 μg/mL, red). Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue). Scale bar indicates 50 μm for fluorescence microscopy and 10 μm for confocal microscopy. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Characterization of PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX. (a) Size distribution of PRCDs. (b) Absorption (blue) spectrum, fluorescence emission spectra in water (red) and in EtOH (black) of PRCDs respectively. (c) FTIR spectrum of RCDs, PRCDs and PRCD@DTX respectively. (d) Zeta potential of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX respectively (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

DTX loading efficiency and release behavior study. (a) DTX loading efficiency of PRCD@DTX with different drug dosing amount (n = 3). (b) A pH responsive release of DTX from PRCD@DTX in corresponding buffer at pH 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4 respectively (n = 3).

Targeting study of HPRCD@DTX. The fluorescence microscopy images of SKBR-3 cells incubated with RCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX (red) in (a)10-fold field and (b) 20-fold field (the concentration of DTX was 60 μg/mL). Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue). Scale bar indicates 50 μm. (c) The red fluorescence quantification of SKBR-3 cells treated with RCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX in 10-fold field (n = 3). (d) The red fluorescence quantification of SKBR-3 cells treated with RCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX in 20-fold field (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Content of DTX uptake by SKBR-3 cells treated with free DTX, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX respectively (DTX concentration is 60 μg/mL). (n = 3).

Pharmaceutical activity test. (a) Viability of MCF-7 and SKBR-3 cells after incubation with free DTX at different DTX concentration (blue bars for MCF-7 cells and red bars for SKBR-3 cells). (b) Viability of SKBR-3 cells after incubation with PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX at different DTX concentration (orange bars for PRCD@DTX and red bars for HPRCD@DTX). (c) Viability of MCF-7 and SKBR-3 cells after incubation with HPRCD@DTX at different DTX concentration (blue bars for MCF-7 cells and red bars for SKBR-3 cells). (d) Viability of SKBR-3 cells after incubation with HPRCD@DTX in different human environment (pink bar for pH 5.5, red bar for pH 6.5 and blue bar for pH 7.4 respectively). *P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.001 (n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The flow cytometric analyses of apoptotic cell with Annexin V-FITC and PI staining in SKBR-3 cells for 12-h incubation with HPRCD@DTX. (a–c) Heatmap of apoptosis of SKBR3 cells treated with HPRCD@DTX (pH 5.5, pH 6.5 and pH 7.4 respectively). (d–f) Statistical histogram of apoptosis results of SKBR3 cells treated with HPRCD@DTX (early, late and total apoptosis respectively) (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX synthesis process and treating process of HPRCD@DTX towards HER2 – positive breast cancer.

head.ce:title: Innovative tumor interstitial fluid-triggered carbon dot-docetaxel nanoassemblies for targeted drug delivery and imaging of HER2-positive breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Health Science Center, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710061, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Health Science Center, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis, Xi’an 710061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis, Xi’an 710061, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Xi’an Jiaotong University, 76 Yanta West Road, Xi’an 710061, Shaanxi, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have developed an innovative pH-triggered nanomedicine delivery system, targeting HER2-positive breast cancer cells for effective low-cost, imaging-guided drug delivery and precise therapy. The key feature of this system lies in its unique tumor interstitial fluid microenvironment-responsive drug release behavior which achieved tumor site-specific drug delivery. Our in vitro experiments demonstrated that the carbon dot-integrated material achieves more efficient DTX release (96.13 % at 72 h) in the tumor interstitial fluid microenvironment (pH 6.5), thereby boosting drug concentration at the tumor site and enhancing therapeutic efficacy. Further cell experiments confirmed the system's significant inhibitory effect on HER2-positive tumor cells SKBR3 in a pH 6.5 environment, and apoptosis assays indicating a notable increase in early cell apoptosis (from 8.39 % to 24.61 % compared with pH 7.4). Furthermore, the integration of HER2 aptamer within the carbon dot-based system enables targeted recognition and binding to tumor cells, ensuring more precise delivery of DTX while minimizing potential side effects. Crucially, the carbon dots in this system emit superior red fluorescence (the QY = 47.64 % excited at 535 nm compared with Rodamine 6G), enabling real-time visualization of the drug delivery process. This feature provides valuable feedback on treatment effectiveness, facilitating necessary adjustments. The small size (1.88 ± 0.48 nm) of carbon dots significantly improved their ability to penetrate biological barriers, while their low toxicity (no significant cell toxicity under 350 μg/mL) contributed to the formulation’s outstanding biocompatibility. Overall, this carbon dot-enhanced drug delivery system offers immense potential for enhancing drug efficacy, minimizing side effects, and providing real-time treatment monitoring, thus proposing a innovate strategy for breast cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: HER2-positive breast cancer, defined by the amplification or overexpression of HER2/neu, a key receptor tyrosine kinase in the epidermal growth factor receptor family (Adrienne and Eric, 2019; Iqbal and Iqbal, 2014). This subtype represents approximately 20 % of breast cancer cases (Adrienne and Eric, 2019; Larissa et al., 2021) and is associated with poor prognosis in the absence of systemic therapy. Current clinical therapies for HER2-positive breast cancer include anti-ERBB2 antibodies (trastuzumab and pertuzumab) and small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitors (lapatinib and neratinib) (Fan et al., 2014). While these drugs are effective, they frequently come with high costs and severe side effects, including damage to healthy tissues. To address these issues, several HER2-targeting aptamers have been synthesized as a low-cost, stable, and specific alternative. Aptamers such as HApt, Apt, and HB5 have shown promising prospects in targeting drug delivery (Chowdhury et al., 2020; Ghassami et al., 2019; Heo et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2017; Niazi et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2017; Powell et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020). As a result, various drug-loaded nanoparticles have been developed and modified with HER2 aptamers (Nguyen et al., 2017; Powell et al., 2017), leveraging their specific recognition and binding capabilities to enable targeted chemotherapy delivery to tumor sites.

To enhance drug delivery efficiency, various trigger-release drug carrier systems, including temperature, ultrasoud or pH-triggering, have been developed (Kanamala et al., 2016; Zhu and Chen, 2015). Traditionally targeting the acidic tumor microenvironment, these systems typically focus on pH levels such as 5.0 or 5.5 (Chen et al., 2017; Mewada et al., 2014), contrasting with the normal tissue pH of 7.4. However, recent research reveals a more refined pH gradient within tumors. Specifically, intracellular tumor environments and lysosomes have a pH around 5.5, while tumor interstitial fluid and nearby solid tumor areas are about pH 6.5 (Alvarez-Lorenzo and Concheiro, 2014; Kong et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). This distinct pH levels are crucial for developing more precise, pH-responsive drug carriers, enabling targeted and efficient drug release at specific tumor sites.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become recognized as a significant carbon-based material, known for their applications in imaging (Liu et al., 2019) and drug delivery due to their favorable optical properties, high drug loading capacity, easy surface functionalization, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Liu et al., 2020a,b; Panwar et al., 2019). The characteristics of CDs have enabled their use in imaging-guided chemotherapy drugs delivery (Bao et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2018; Jin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a,b; Lu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2013) and phototherapy (Shu et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2019; Williams et al., 1983) for the treatment of cancers. The use of CDs in imaging-guided drug delivery systems allows therapists to monitor the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion of drugs in real-time, providing greater insight into the treatment process and improving the safety of medication. With this technology, therapists can optimize drug dosages and delivery routes for maximum effectiveness and minimal side effects.

In this study, we developed a nanomedicine delivery system HPRCD@DTX, aimed specifically at tumor extracellular microenvironment of HER2-positive breast cancer. The system comprises a HER2 aptamer, polyethylene glycol (PEG), red carbon dots (RCDs), and docetaxel (DTX). A key feature of HPRCD@DTX is its pH-triggered drug release, particularly effective in the tumor interstitial fluid (pH 6.5) and tumor endochylema (pH 5.5) microenvironments. This characteristic guarantees enhanced drug release at the tumor site, thereby increasing its concentration and amplifying its therapeutic effect. Furthermore, the red carbon dots in this system offer distinct red fluorescence, facilitating real-time monitoring of the drug delivery process. This imaging feature is essential in directing the treatment of HER2-positive breast cancer. Lastly, the HER2 aptamer facilitates targeted delivery to cancer cells through specific binding to HER2 receptors, thereby boosting treatment efficacy and minimizing side effects. Collectively, HPRCD@DTX represents a promising approach for precise, imaging-guided cancer therapy, integrating efficient drug delivery with real-time monitoring of treatment.

All chemical reagents were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (China) and solvents were purchased from Hubei FTSCI BioTech Co., Ltd (China). HER2 aptamers (HApt, 5′-GCA GCG GTG TGG GGG CAG CGG TGT GGG GGC AGC GGT GTG GGG-3′) with a NH2 modified at 3′ were synthesized by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. (China). Cell culture reagents were bought from Corning (USA). SKBR-3 and MCF-7 cell were purchased from Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd (China).

Human breast cancer cell line MCF-7 (HER2 − lowexpressed breast cancer cell) and SKBR-3 (HER2 − positive breast cancer cell) were cultured at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 condition. MCF-7 cells were cultured using DMEM medium supplemented with fetal bovine serum (10 %, v/v), and penicillin–streptomycin (1 %, v/v), SKBR-3 cells were cultured with Mycco’s 5A medium supplemented with fetal bovine serum (10 %, v/v), and penicillin–streptomycin (1 %, v/v).

Initially, red fluorescence carbon dots (RCDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. O-Phenylenediamine (3.0 mg), urea (9.0 mg), and 30 mL of water were mixed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the reaction mixture was washed 3 times and dried in a lyophilizer. Subsequently, the dried raw product was purified using column chromatography with methanol and trichloromethane as eluents.

For the synthesis of PRCDs, 1.0 mg of COOH-PEG-COOH (MW = 1000 kDa) was dissolved in 2 mL of water, followed by dropwise addition to a 1 mg/mL RCD solution. The PRCDs solution underwent sonication for 15 min and was then stirred in an 80 °C water bath for 12 h before 1.2 mg of DTX dissolved in 0.5 mL of methanol was introduced. Following overnight shaking at 37 °C, the mixture underwent dialysis for 8 h to remove unbound RCDs and untrapped DTX. The resulting product, PRCD@DTX, was then freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

For the synthesis of the HER2-targeted HPRCD@DTX nanoassembly, 2.5 mg of PRCD@DTX, 2.5 mg of EDC, and 5.0 mg of NHS were combined in 5 mL of PBS. This mixture was then subjected to ultrasound for 30 min to activate the carboxyl groups and create a homogeneous suspension. A volume of 62.5 μL of NH2-HApt (50 μM) was then added to the PRCD@DTX suspension (the ratio of HApt to PRCD@DTX was determined from pre-experiments to be 0.2 mg of PRCD@DTX for every 5 μL of HApt, as shown in Fig. S1) and gently agitated overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently, the product was purified using an Amicon-3K (Merck Millipore, catalog No. UFC500324, Billerica, MA, USA) centrifugation filter concentrator, applying PBS and performing the procedure three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX, HPRCD@DTX and the QY determination of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectroscopic measurements were conducted on a RF6000 spectro fluorophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra were recorded with a TU-1810PC ultraviolet absorption photometer (Beijing General Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd, China). Carbon dot morphology was examined using a JEM 2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Zeta potential and size distribution were characterized using a Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was performed on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Shaanxi Yingmei Electronic Technology Co., Ltd, China) within the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1. Flow cytometry analysis was conducted using an ACEA NovoCyte (Agilent, USA). DTX content was determined using an LC-2040C 3D high-performance liquid chromatography system (SHIMADZU, Japan).

The quantum yield (QY) of fluorescence for RCDs was determined using the methodology in the literature (Sun et al., 2019). This involved measuring the fluorescence intensities of both the sample and standard across a range of concentrations, using the same excitation wavelength. Six samples and six standards were prepared with absorbances set at 0 (solvent blank), 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.10, respectively, measured at 535 nm. The fluorescence QY of RCDs was calculated using the equation below:
Φ X
=
Φ ST
Grad X
Grad ST
η
X
2
η
ST
2
where
Φ X
and
Φ ST
represent the fluorescence QY of RCDs and standard (Rhodamine 6G) in ethanol respectively. Gradx and GradST represent the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vesus absorbance, and
η X
and
η ST
is the refractive index of the solvent.

To evaluate the imaging capabilities of RCDs, SKBR-3 cells were incubated with RCDs (200 μg/mL) and DAPI (500 ng/mL) in both a 24-well microplate (5 × 104 cells per well) and in a confocal dish (1 × 104 cells). Fluorescence microscopy and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) were utilized to visualize the red and blue fluorescence emitted by RCDs and DAPI, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DTX loading efficiency and release behavior of PRCD@DTX
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of the DTX loading capacity for the nanocomplex proceeded as follows: Initially, varying amounts of DTX (3.0 mg, 6.0 mg, 9.0 mg, 15.0 mg, and 24.0 mg, each dissolved in 4.0 mL methanol) were added dropwise to a solution containing 6.0 mg of PRCDs in 6 mL of methanol. The mixture was then kept stirring at 80 °C overnight, subjected to 8 h of dialysis, and subsequently lyophilized. After lyophilize, same amount of PRCD@DTX sample was treated with 2 mL of methanol and subjected to ultrasound for 1 h to ensure complete DTX extraction. And the DTX loading efficiency (DLE%) was determined by the formula: DLE% = WDTX/WTotal × 100 %, where WTotal and WDTX represent weight of PRCD@DTX and DTX respectively.

The in vitro release of DTX from PRCD@DTX was evaluated using buffers at pH levels of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, corresponding to the endocytotic environment of cancer cells, tumor interstitial fluid, and normal physiological conditions, respectively. These pH levels were selected based on findings from previous research (Han et al., 2016; Jia et al., 2016; Kong et al., 2018).

Specifically, 2.0 mg of PRCD@DTX was dissolved in 2 mL of PBS at pH 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, respectively. Each resulting solution was then sealed within a dialysis bag and submerged in 100 mL of its respective buffer. The release system was kept stirring at 37 °C at a rate of 200 rpm/min. Samples (1 mL) were withdrawn from the release medium at predetermined time intervals (0, 5 min, 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, and 72 h) for HPLC analysis, and an equal volume (1 mL) of the fresh corresponding buffer was replenished to each dialysis bag.

The aptamer anchored on the PRCD@DTX surface allows the nanoassembly to specifically target HER2-positive cell membranes. To validate the targeting ability of HPRCD@DTX, SKBR-3 cells were seeded in a 24-well microplate at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with RCDs, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX (each at CDTX = 60 μg/mL) for 8 h at 37 °C in darkness. Following the removal of the culture medium, cells were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde and their nuclei stained with DAPI (500 ng/mL). Finally, fluorescence images for RCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX were captured using a fluorescence microscope and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM).

To examine the intracellular uptake of free DTX, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX, SKBR-3 cells were plated in 12-well plates at 2 × 105 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to varying concentrations (20–80 μg/mL) of free DTX, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX. Following a 12-hour incubation, the cells were harvested and lysed using 200 μL of RIPA buffer. Upon adding 1 mL of methanol to each sample, the mixtures were sonicated for one hour and subsequently centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for five minutes. The supernatants were collected for DTX quantification by HPLC.

The CCK-8 assay was employed to evaluate the pharmacological activity. The experiments with free DTX, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX followed identical protocols. MCF-7 and SKBR-3 cells were inoculated in a 96-well microplate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to treatments of DTX, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX, each at concentrations ranging from 10-60 μg/mL, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The subsequent procedures mirrored those used in the cytotoxicity assay.

To further assess the inhibitory effects of HPRCD@DTX on SKBR-3 cells under different pH conditions, additional pharmacological assays were performed. SKBR-3 cells were uniformly seeded into a 96-well microplate at a specified density and incubated for 24 h. HPRCD@DTX solutions (20 μg/mL) were prepared by adjusting the pH of McCoy's 5A medium to 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, respectively, and dissolving the designated amount of HPRCD@DTX in each medium. The SKBR-3 cells were subsequently treated with the HPRCD@DTX solutions, maintaining a constant incubation temperature of 37 °C for 24 h. The following procedures mirrored the methodology employed in the cytotoxicity assay.

SKBR-3 cells were plated in 6-well plates at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to HPRCD@DTX at varying pH levels (pH 5.5, pH 6.5, and pH 7.4), with a DTX concentration of 20 μg/mL for 12 h. After treatment, cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC and propidium iodide (PI) in the dark. Flow cytometry was employed to quantify the percentage of apoptotic cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, characterization, and spectroscopic properties of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX
ce:section.ce:para: Recent research indicates that carbon dots co-doped with heteroatoms typically exhibit longer-wavelength light emission. This emission wavelength significantly depends on the precursor selection and the specific heteroatoms introduced, notably nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) (Jiang et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2017). Additionally, carbon dots derived from aromatic compound precursors have demonstrated emission in the red/near-infrared (NIR) spectrum or exhibited multicolor emissions (Ding et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2016). In light of these findings, o-phenylenediamine and urea were chosen as precursors to synthesize red-emitting carbon dots via the hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to analyze the morphology and particle size of the obtained red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs), revealing them to be quasi-spherical, monodisperse, and uniform in size. The size distribution of the RCDs ranged from 1.0 to 4.0 nm, with an average diameter of 1.88 ± 0.48 nm (n = 100, as depicted in the left inset of Fig. 1 a). Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image displayed clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.15 nm, as illustrated in the right inset of Fig. 1a.

The UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of the RCDs (Fig. 1b) exhibits a broad absorption band ranging from 500 nm to 600 nm. The red line represents the fluorescence emission spectrum of RCDs in ethanol, demonstrating two pronounced peaks at 600 nm and 650 nm upon excitation at 535 nm or 550 nm. Conversely, the black line represents the fluorescence emission spectrum of RCDs in water, revealing a single peak at 620 nm under the same excitation wavelengths as those in ethanol. Emission above 600 nm underscores the effective red emission of RCDs, which is crucial for minimizing interference from the inherent blue fluorescence of biological tissues. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the RCDs, excited at 535 nm in ethanol, was determined to be 47.64 %, with Rhodamine 6G serving as the standard. Linear plots comparing RCDs and Rhodamine 6G are presented in Fig. 1c, demonstrating the desirable red fluorescence of RCDs, which is suitable for use as a cell dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was employed to identify the primary functional groups present in the RCDs, as shown in Fig. 1d. The peak at 737 cm−1 corresponds to the CH bending vibration associated with ortho-substitution on the benzene ring, while the peak at 1654 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CC. The peaks at 2911 cm−1 and 3032 cm−1 attributed to the CH vibration in aromatic hydrocarbons, and the peak at 3135 cm−1 derives from cumulative CC. These spectral features indicate the presence of aromatic rings with ortho-substitutions in the RCDs. The peaks at 1198 cm−1 and 1272 cm−1 originate from the CH and CN vibrations of aliphatic amines, respectively, and the peak at 1654 cm−1 is due to the NH stretching vibration of an amide. These three peaks suggest that NH2 groups are attached to aliphatic carbon within the RCDs. Meanwhile, the peak at 1743 cm−1 derives from the stretching vibration of CO, indicating the presence of a CONH group in the RCDs.

The imaging results of SKBR-3 cells stained with RCDs and DAPI are depicted in Fig. 1e. Both fluorescence microscopy and confocal microscopy images demonstrate that the entire cell is permeated with the red fluorescence of RCDs, and lysosomes display a stronger fluorescence intensity. These results underscore the exceptional cell imaging capabilities of RCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PRCDs, PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX
ce:section.ce:para: To modify the surface and control the size of RCDs, COOH-PEG-COOH was employed. The resulting PEGylated RCDs (PRCDs) exhibited an average size of 19.60 ± 1.43 nm, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. Fig. 2b shows the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the PRCDs. An absorption band from 500 nm to 600 nm was observed in the spectra, with a notably reduced intensity. This reduction is likely attributed to the presence of PEG molecules on the RCDs surface. Additionally, under the same excitation wavelengths (535 nm, 550 nm), the fluorescence emission exhibited a strong peak at 620 nm in water. In ethanol (EtOH), a strong peak was observed at 600 nm and a very weak peak at 650 nm.

The FTIR spectra of RCDs, PRCDs, and PRCD@DTX (Fig. 2c) exhibited commonalities including peaks at 2911 cm−1, 1743 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, and 1272 cm−1, mirroring those found in RCDs. Unlike the spectrum of RCDs, a peak at 3500 cm−1 in the spectra of PRCDs and PRCD@DTX indicates the presence of the OH group, a result of the COOH-PEG-COOH modification.

Fig. 2d shows the zeta potential measurements of RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX. The measured zeta potential values for RCDs, PRCDs, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX were 4.68 mV, −7.79 mV, −14.7 mV, and −21.6 mV, respectively. Following the surface modification and the electrostatic interaction-based drug loading process, the zeta potential value decreased to −21.6 mV, This reduction strongly suggests the successful formation of the HPRCD@DTX nanoassembly and indicates a trend toward particle stabilization. Additionally, colloidal stability testing revealed that the HPRCD@DTX solution remains stable for up to 24 h, as shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DTX loading efficiency and pH-Triggered DTX release from PRCD@DTX in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The loading efficiency of PRCD@DTX was evaluated by adding varying amounts of DTX to a fixed quantity of PRCDs, using four distinct ratios: 50 %, 100 %, 150 %, and 400 %. The final drug loading efficiency (DLE) was determined by HPLC. Fig. 3 a demonstrates that the DLE of PRCD@DTX followed a parabolic trend, peaking at a drug ratio of 150 %. However, due to the lack of statistical significance between the DLEs at drug ratios of 100 % and 150 %, we selected the 100 % drug ratio for subsequent experiments, resulting in a DLE of 57.63 ± 3.12 %.

Effective drug delivery is essential in cancer treatment, and the acidic tumor microenvironment has been shown to facilitate controlled drug release. Further investigation was conducted in vitro on the release of DTX under different pH conditions using PBS with 0.5 % Tween80, at pH values of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, which were used to simulate the tumor endochylema of cancer cells, interstitial fluid of cancer, and physiological environment, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3b, DTX release rates increased over time and stabilized after roughly 36 h across all pH levels. However, the cumulative release rate of DTX at pH 6.5 (89.25 %) was much higher than that at pH 5.5 (43.65 %) and pH 7.4 (34.09 %).

The differential release rates between pH 5.5 and 7.4 might be attributed to the following reasons: Protonation, a key mechanism for pH-triggered drug delivery, involves the use of polymers with ionizable groups, like carboxyl groups, to construct nanocarriers. In our study, we modified the carbon dots using COOH-PEG-COOH. Due to the high concentration of protonatable carboxyl groups, PRCD@DTX underwent protonation in a mildly acidic environment (pH 6.5), which led to structural transformation and the release of DTX. Secondly, the strong hydrogen bonding forces of the NH2 groups present on the surface of RCDs made them resistant to structural transformation in a more acidic environment with a pH of 5.5, which ultimately led to a lower release of DTX. Thirdly, under neutral pH conditions, PRCD@DTX remained deprotonated, resulting in the retention of most of the DTX within its core and causing the lowest release of DTX among the three tested pH values.

The tumor microenvironment-responsive drug release profile of PRCD@DTX enabled optimal release in the cancer cell interstitial fluid (pH 6.5), moderate release in the tumor endochylema (pH 5.5), and minimized release in neutral conditions (pH 7.4). This selective drug release profile can potentially reduce systemic side effects and improve the therapeutic outcomes of targeted chemotherapy drug delivery to tumor tissues.

To evaluate the targeting ability of HPRCD@DTX on SKBR-3 cells, RCDs, PRCD@DTX, and HPRCD@DTX (CDTX = 60 μg/mL) were incubated with SKBR-3 cells for 8 h before conducting fluorescence imaging. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, b, the HPRCD@DTX group exhibited significantly stronger red fluorescence compared to the PRCD@DTX group, with the fluorescence notably concentrated on the cell membrane. Conversely, the red fluorescence from the RCDs and PRCD@DTX groups was uniformly distributed across SKBR-3 cells. These findings demonstrate that HPRCD@DTX possesses precise targeting capabilities for the HER2 protein on the SKBR-3 cell membrane.

To evaluate the intracellular uptake of DTX, SKBR-3 cells were subjected to treatment with PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX, followed by lysis. Subsequently, DTX was extracted with methanol and quantified via HPLC. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the uptake patterns of free DTX and PRCD@DTX were similar, with PRCD@DTX displaying controlled release behavior, leading to reduced DTX release at equivalent time intervals. Specifically, the uptake amount for the free DTX group (80 μg/mL) was 1.95 ± 0.31 μg, compared to 1.55 ± 0.19 μg for the PRCD@DTX group (80 μg/mL) However, after surface modification with the aptamer HApt, there was a significant increase in uptake compared to the free DTX and PRCD@DTX groups, attributable to the specific interaction between HApt and the HER2 protein on the SKBR-3 cell membrane. The uptake amount for HPRCD@DTX (80 μg/mL) reached 2.97 ± 0.14 μg. Notably, HPRCD@DTX showed significantly higher uptake levels than both free DTX and PRCD@DTX across all tested concentrations (40, 60, 80 μg/mL), confirming the enhanced uptake due to HApt targeting on SKBR-3 cells.

Fig. 6 a shows that the survival rates of MCF-7 and SKBR-3 cells decreased dose-dependently after DTX treatment. Notably, at a DTX concentration of 60 μg/mL, MCF-7 cell viability reduced to 49.74 %, whereas SKBR-3 cells maintained higher viability at 60.14 % under free DTX treatment. This suggests SKBR-3 cells have greater drug resistance than MCF-7 cells, indicating DTX may be less effective on SKBR-3 cells, requiring additional strategies to improve therapeutic outcomes.

Fig. 6b presents the survival rates of SKBR-3 cells following treatment with PRCD@DTX and HPRCD@DTX. The results show that HPRCD@DTX, incorporating the HER2 aptamer HApt, exhibits enhanced targeting towards SKBR-3 cells (viability from 63.12 % to 48.92 %, DTX 60 μg/mL).

Fig. 6c reveals that HPRCD@DTX exhibits increased cytotoxicity against SKBR-3 cells compared to MCF-7, with DTX concentrations ranging from 20 to 60 μg/mL (IC50 = 54.44 μg/mL). This suggests that HPRCD@DTX has a more potent anti-cancer effect on SKBR-3 cells. Specifically, with DTX at 60 μg/mL, cell viability was 48.92 % for SKBR-3 and 71.83 % for MCF-7, highlighting HPRCD@DTX’s superior targeting of SKBR-3 cells.

Fig. 6d displays the cell viability of SKBR-3 cells treated with HPRCD@DTX (DTX 20 μg/mL) under various simulated human body environments, including the endocytotic environment of cancer cells (pH 5.5), the interstitial fluid of tumors (pH 6.5), and normal physiological conditions (pH 7.4). The findings reveal that SKBR-3 cells had the lowest survival rate at pH 6.5 (45.62 %) compared to pH 5.5 (67.03 %) and the highest at pH 7.4 (72.79 %). These results suggest that HPRCD@DTX exhibits the most potent anti-cancer effect in a pH 6.5 environment, indicating its maximum anti-cancer potential is realized in the tumor interstitial fluid microenvironment.

The ability of HPRCD@DTX to induce apoptosis in SKBR-3 cells across different pH levels was evaluated using flow cytometry with the Annexin V-FITC apoptosis detection kit. As depicted in Fig. 7 , SKBR-3 cells were classified into four categories: necrotic, intact, early apoptotic, and late apoptotic cells. Apoptotic ratios for the groups at pH 5.5, pH 6.5, and pH 7.4 were 14.43 %, 24.61 %, and 8.39 %, respectively. Total apoptosis (combining early and late stages) for these groups were 25.60 %, 31.73 %, and 16.29 %, respectively. These results indicate that HPRCD@DTX most effectively induces apoptosis in SKBR-3 cells in the tumor interstitial fluid environment (pH 6.5). I within this environment, the rates of early and total apoptosis were 2.9-fold and 1.9-fold higher, respectively, than at pH 7.4.

In summary, we have successfully developed the innovative, imaging-guided nanoassembly HPRCD@DTX. This system is specifically designed to target HER2 and release the therapeutic drug, docetaxel (DTX), in a pH-sensitive manner, thereby enhancing treatment efficacy. Our findings demonstrate that HPRCD@DTX exhibits excellent biocompatibility, effective fluorescence properties, high drug loading capacity, and sensitivity to the tumor interstitial fluid environment. Under neutral conditions, HPRCD@DTX maintains stability, with maximum release observed in the slightly acidic tumor environment (pH 6.5) and substantial release also noted in the endocytic environment of cancer cells (pH 5.5), thereby effectively minimizing side effects on healthy cells. Additionally, HPRCD@DTX's selective and targeted binding to HER2-positive breast cancer cells has been confirmed, highlighting its dual functionality as both a therapeutic agent and a tool for real-time monitoring of drug dynamics. Furthermore, our in vitro studies show that HPRCD@DTX achieves superior cellular uptake and exhibits enhanced inhibitory effects on HER2-positive breast cancer cells. This underscores its significant potential as both a targeted cancer therapeutic and an imaging-guided diagnostic agent.

Dan Xu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Dongnan Guo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jing Zhang: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xueping Tan: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zijie Deng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaofang Hou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Sicen Wang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.82273891, 81973277, 82003709), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M643681) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No: 2021JM-039) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2024.124145.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
